**Welcome to “Sword World”!** 

4

## **What is a Role-Playing Game (RPG)?** 

_Role-Playing_ means _to act out a role_ . A role-playing game (RPG), as the name suggests, progresses by having each participant in the game play out an assigned _role_ . 

Participants in an RPG can be roughly divided into two groups: One is the **game master** ( **GM** ), while the other are the **players** . RPGs are played by several people, but there is only one game master while the rest are the players. 

Before playing a game, the game master must prepare what’s called a **scenario** . You may either create your own, or you may use an existing one. A number of scenario collections have already been produced for _Sword World RPG_ , and will continue to be released in the future. 

The game proceeds according to a prepared scenario. The game master is the one who facilitates the scenario, while the players _play the role_ -- of the **characters** --in the scenario. If you think of the game master as a director and the players as actors, it's similar to making a movie or a play. 

However, there is something that’s very different from film or theater. Which is: the players do _not_ know the scenario. They _only_ know the characters they’re taking on.  The players must make their characters act based on their abilities and characteristics. The game master's scenario does _not_ provide any dialogue, etc., for the characters. 

Let's give a real example. **GM** is the game master, and **A** , **B** , and **C** are different players. 

**GM** ： You guys were traveling and you arrived at a small village. You're now resting at a tavern now that the sun is setting. Then, about five or six unsavorylooking people enter. They start to have fun by making nasty remarks at the 

poster girl at the bar, much to her annoyance. 

**A** ： That doesn't sound good... okay, I'll give them a little warning. 

**B** ： Come on, if you get yourself hurt, you'll look like a fool. Just ignore them. 

**A** ： What do you mean? That would soil the name of a hero of justice! 

**B** ： You're the only one who thinks he’s a hero of justice, aren't you?” 

**C** ： .......say, is the poster girl pretty? 

**GM** ： She is. 

**C** ： What do the thugs look like? 

**GM** ： They have very dirty leather armor and shortswords. 

**B** ： They sound like bandits. That Thieves Guild scares me. 

**C** ： Even I have the backing of the Thieves Guild (laughs). These guys don't seem like a big bunch, so I say we be "heroes of justice". Hey you guys, leave her alone! 

**A** ： That's my line! 

**B** ： I’ll just pretend I’m not with you guys… 

**GM** ： But you're all sitting at the same table, so I don’t think they’ll believe you. (laughs) One of the thugs, who looks particularly rough, snarls “What, you have the gall to complain about us - the Brando family?!”. 

**B** ： Uh-oh... we're in trouble… 

**A** ： Bring it on! 

Thus, the game proceeds. In this case, the scenario prepared by the game master does _not_ say _there will be a fight with thugs (the Brando family)_ . It only says _thugs will misbehave in the tavern_ , then it's up to the players to decide what they'll do about it. If the players were all like player **B** , there would've been no conflict. The conflict only arose because 

the players wanted it. So, what will be the outcome of this fight? 

Here is where another major feature of role-playing games comes into play. That is… 

Not knowing what the results will be. 

That is to say: 

Neither the game master nor the players know whether the characters will be able to kick the thugs out of the tavern and become _heroes of justice_ , or get beaten by the thugs and instead become _heroes of comedy_ . 

Even in real life, if you start a fight, you won't know who will win without actually fighting. The only thing you should know is _Who is considered stronger?_ . 

Role-playing games reproduce this uncertainty through random numbers (mainly dice) and numerical values representing character attributes. 

In other words, based on a number that represents a character's strength, a result is determined by rolling the dice. 

Sometimes it’s like this: 

**Player** ： (rolls) Hey, I got a good roll, my attack is a hit! 

Other times, it’s like this: 

**Player** ： (rolls) Agh, my roll was bad. My attack failed! 

In this way, due to two types of randomness, _player's choice_ and _random numbers (dice)_ , the actual game can display a wide degree of fluctuation, sometimes the "story" will evolve beyond the expectations of the game master. 

In a role-playing game, you can create a story, as well as enjoy the story as a spectator. 

If you enjoy the story in this way, you can say that you've experienced the best part of a role-playing game. 

5

## **The Meaning of the Rulebook** 

This book is a rulebook for the roleplaying game entitled _Sword World RPG_ . 

As stated, a role-playing game progresses according to a scenario prepared by the game master, as well as the players' decisions and random numbers. This rulebook is here to help you with that. 

A role-playing game, like most fiction, takes place in a fictional world. As mentioned earlier, Forcelia, the world you’ll deal with in _Sword World RPG_ , is different from our real world. There is magic, and creatures that can only be found in fiction will come to life. 

However, even fictional worlds have their own laws. _Because it's magic_ does not mean something is all-powerful, and _because they're monsters_ does not mean all monsters are equally strong or scary. _Because you're a hero_ doesn't mean you can do everything. Each has their own characteristics and limitations. 

For example, some people may have spectacular muscle strength, while others may be less fortunate. A weapon wielded by someone with superior strength can do more damage than a weapon wielded by someone who is not blessed with such strength. However, with training and experience, even those with inferior talents should be able to fight admirably. 

To the average person, a tiger would be a terrifying creature. However, the 

The rules set forth in this book are the fundaments that characterize Forcelia. If you don't understand them, you won't be able to grasp the characteristics of Forcelia, but not _all_ of Forcelia's laws are written in this book. Please understand this. In making decisions, be sure to add your own _common sense_ to the rules. 

heroes present in _Sword World RPG_ are capable of fighting as well as or even better than a tiger. 

The rules exist to reproduce such things. To what extent does a person's muscular aptitude affect their ability to fight? 

How much does experience make up for it? How do tigers, ordinary people, and heroes differ in strength? 

It should be noted that, as a general principle of role-playing games, the game master makes the final decisions. In all cases, the word of the game master is absolute in the progress of the game. You may think of the rules as the _law_ and the game master as the _judge_ who applies it. 

It is the _rules_ that represent that. 

In this sense, the rulebook is written to reproduce all the laws and details in the fictional world. If decisions are made according to the rulebook, success or failure of the characters' abilities and actions in the fictional world can all be determined with certainty. 


![](harness/cache/e5870dca757ef182/images/Sword_World_RPG_-_Complete_Edition.pdf-0006-14.jpg)


**----- Start of picture text -----**<br>
is<br>.<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


Unfortunately, however, this is merely an _ideal_ . 

For example, it was stated that Forcelia follows different laws than the real world. Indeed, they are different in many ways. Therefore, in order to be a complete rulebook, _all_ of its laws would have to be written. That is to say, _an apple that falls from a tree lands on the ground_ . 

This is neither possible nor realistic. Rather, it's better to think of the rules set forth by the book as merely a guide. For things that the rules cannot fully define, please use your own **common sense** to make decisions. Even though it's not written in the book, _an apple that falls from a tree lands on the ground._ 

6

## **The Structure of the Rulebook** 

This book consists of five major parts. 


![](harness/cache/e5870dca757ef182/images/Sword_World_RPG_-_Complete_Edition.pdf-0007-02.jpg)


## **Welcome to Sword World!** 

This is the part you are reading right now. It's a brief introduction to _Sword World RPG_ . If you're not familiar with role-playing games or _Sword World RPG_ , please start reading here. 


![](harness/cache/e5870dca757ef182/images/Sword_World_RPG_-_Complete_Edition.pdf-0007-05.jpg)


## **Part 1 Player Section** 

This is the part for the players, one of the two groups of people needed to enjoy an RPG. It introduces the minimum rules you should remember when playing _Sword World RPG_ . 


![](harness/cache/e5870dca757ef182/images/Sword_World_RPG_-_Complete_Edition.pdf-0007-08.jpg)


## **Part 2 Game Master Section** 

This is the part for the game master. A game master requires a deeper mastery of an RPG compared to players. When you’re a game master, you will have to remember rules that you wouldn’t have to remember when you’re a player. Rules that aren’t necessary from a player's point of view are included here. 

Therefore, if you're solely a player, you _don't_ have to force yourself to memorize the rules of this part. Conversely, a game master _must_ understand the rules included here in addition to those included in Part 1. 


![](harness/cache/e5870dca757ef182/images/Sword_World_RPG_-_Complete_Edition.pdf-0007-12.jpg)


## **Part 3 Optional Rules** 

These are rules designed to make playing _Sword World RPG_ more interesting. However, having additional rules to memorize often gets in the way 

of the speedy development that is the life of an RPG. 

The rules in this section are for those who already have an understanding of _Sword World RPG_ and are able to play with ease. It's _not_ recommended for inexperienced players to use the rules here from the beginning. 


![](harness/cache/e5870dca757ef182/images/Sword_World_RPG_-_Complete_Edition.pdf-0007-17.jpg)


## **Guide to Forcelia and Alecrast** 

_Sword World RPG_ is set on the continent of **Alecrast** , a part of the world called **Forcelia** . This part is a brief overview of Forcelia as a whole, the history of Alecrast, and various regions. 

In order to play an RPG, it's best to have all participants understand the background setting, and proceed with the game implicitly based on this **common understanding** . This part should help you with that. 

## **What You’ll Need to Play Sword World RPG** 

When playing _Sword World RPG_ , you will have to write and rewrite many things during the game. So you’ll need pencils, erasers, notepads, etc. 

You will also need Character Sheets. A Character Sheet is used to keep track of a character who acts in a fictional world (Alecrast in the case of _Sword World RPG_ ) as the player's alter ego. These are needed per the number of people. Please copy and use the sheets included in this book. 

other RPGs, incorporates chance and uses dice to resolve various actions, in order to make the game more fun. The dice used are ordinary six-sided dice. Since two dice are often used at a time, it is advisable to bring two dice per person. 

From here on in this book, rolling dice will be referred to as _rolling 1D_ or _rolling 2D_ . When you _roll 1D_ , roll one die and use the result that comes up. When you _roll 2D_ , roll two dice at the same time and use the total of both numbers (2-12). 

## **Handling fractions** 

Due to the nature of the game, there are situations in _Sword World RPG_ where math calculations must be made. If division results in a fraction, the fraction is either rounded up or rounded down. Which one is clearly stated for each situation. However, if it's _not_ mentioned, the fraction should _always_ be rounded up. 

Along with pens and pencils, you must not forget the dice. As mentioned before, _Sword World RPG_ , like many 

7
